title: What Islam Is All About
description: What Islam Is All About is a religious education by Yahiya Emerick. Banned in 2018 for "promoting enmity among different religious communities".
descriptionParagraphs: What Islam Is All About is a religious education by Yahiya Emerick. Banned in 2018 for "promoting enmity among different religious communities".

What makes it interesting is that interpretation, devotion, satire, or doctrinal conflict becomes a matter of state administration. As a religious education, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in Singapore. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Banned in 2018 for "promoting enmity among different religious communities". More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: What Islam Is All About is a religious education by Yahiya Emerick. Banned in 2018 for "promoting enmity among different religious communities".
banningParagraphs: What Islam Is All About entered censorship debates as a religious education associated with doctrine, public controversy, and religion. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around religious control and religious offense.

The earliest event currently captured here is Date not yet pinned down in Singapore, where Singapore authorities banned publication or circulation. Banned in 2018 for "promoting enmity among different religious communities". Banned in 2018 for "promoting enmity among different religious communities".

counterReadings.title: Assassins of the Mind
counterReadings.author: Christopher Hitchens
counterReadings.note: Frames the Rushdie affair as a test of free speech against violent religious intimidation.

counterReadings.title: From Fatwa to Jihad
counterReadings.author: Kenan Malik
counterReadings.note: Tracks how conflicts over blasphemy, race, and offense evolved after the Rushdie controversy.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
